steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage establishes two parallel facts: Scottish English strongly emphasizes the “r” sound, and Upland South English does the same. It then adds that the Upland South was colonized by people of Scottish ancestry. The word “thus” signals that the blank must be a logical conclusion drawn from those premises.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘the English dialects spoken in the Upland South acquired their emphasis on the “r” sound from dialects spoken in Scotland’: This directly follows from Scottish ancestry of Upland South colonists causing a shared dialect feature — a sound causal conclusion. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘emphasis on the “r” sound will eventually spread from the Upland South to dialects spoken elsewhere’: The passage gives no evidence for future spread; this goes well beyond what the evidence supports. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘the English dialects spoken in Scotland were influenced by dialects spoken in the Upland South’: This reverses the direction of influence. The historical record shows Scotland influencing the Upland South, not the other way around. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘people from Scotland abandoned their emphasis on the “r” sound after relocating to the Upland South’: The passage explicitly states the Upland South strongly emphasizes “r,” contradicting any abandonment of that feature. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
